confidential_patient_information.how_did_you_hear_about_us: Our neighbor, Kelly Thompson, recommended your office. Her son plays on the same soccer team as Ethan and she mentioned that your pediatric chiropractor helped with his posture and recurring headaches. I also looked up your practice on Google afterward and read several positive reviews, especially about working with active kids who play sports.
confidential_patient_information.primary_care_physician: Dr. Melissa Chang, Aurora Pediatric Associates
confidential_patient_information.other_health_professionals_names_and_spe:
Currently followed by:
- Dr. Melissa Chang, MD – General Pediatrics, Aurora Pediatric Associates. She sees Ethan for his annual well-child visits and has evaluated him for his intermittent back pain and headaches.
- Dr. Jason Patel, OD – Pediatric Optometrist, ClearView Kids Vision. Ethan wears glasses for mild astigmatism and sees Dr. Patel once a year for eye exams.
He is not under the ongoing care of any physical therapists, orthopedists, or neurologists at this time.
confidential_patient_information.current_medications_and_supplements:
Ethan does not take any prescription medications on a daily basis.

Over-the-counter / supplements:
- Children’s multivitamin chewable (Flintstones Complete) – 1 tablet daily in the morning with breakfast.
- Children’s chewable probiotic – 1 tablet daily, usually with dinner.
- Children’s acetaminophen (Tylenol) – used only as needed for headaches or fever, about once or twice a month at most, 10 mL dose per his pediatrician’s instructions.
No known medication allergies. No herbal supplements or other remedies at this time.

current_health_conditions.current_health_conditions:
Over the past 8–9 months, Ethan has been having intermittent mid-to-lower back pain and occasional tension-type headaches. The back pain seems to happen most often after soccer practice or games, especially if he has been running hard or playing goalie and diving a lot. He describes it as a tight, achy feeling right in the middle of his back, sometimes a little to the left. On a 0–10 scale, he usually rates it around a 3–5, but once or twice it has gone up to a 6 after a long tournament weekend.

In addition to the back pain, he gets headaches about once a week, usually in the late afternoon or early evening on school days. He tends to rub the back of his neck and the base of his skull when they start. He says it feels like a band of pressure going from the back of his head toward his forehead. The headaches often coincide with days when he’s been on his Chromebook for a longer time at school and then comes home and plays video games or watches YouTube without many breaks.

We have also noticed that his posture has been gradually getting more slouched over the last year. He tends to hunch his shoulders forward when sitting and curls over his tablet or books. His soccer coach mentioned that he sometimes runs slightly leaning forward and that his upper body looks a bit tight when he’s sprinting. At home, he frequently cracks his neck by tilting it side to side, which concerns us.

The pediatrician evaluated him and said it’s likely muscular and posture-related rather than a serious spinal issue, but suggested we look into chiropractic care or physical therapy to help with alignment, flexibility, and posture training. There is no history of major trauma, but he has had a few minor falls during soccer and one fall off a low play structure last spring, where he landed on his bottom and lower back. X-rays at that time were normal. He does not have numbness, tingling, or weakness in his legs, and no problems with bowel or bladder function.

Overall, our main concerns are: recurring mid-to-lower back pain associated with sports and sitting, weekly tension-type headaches that seem connected to neck and upper back tightness, and his increasingly slouched posture, especially while using screens and sitting at school.
current_health_conditions.prior_care_for_condition_explanation:
We first mentioned the back pain and headaches to his pediatrician, Dr. Melissa Chang, during a visit in April 2024. She performed a physical exam, checked his range of motion, reflexes, and strength, and did not find any red flags. She ordered plain X-rays of his lumbar and thoracic spine after a minor fall off a play structure in May 2024; the X-rays were normal.

Dr. Chang recommended conservative care: regular stretching, limiting screen time, improving posture, ensuring his backpack is not too heavy, and using children’s acetaminophen or ibuprofen as needed for pain (which we try to keep minimal). She also suggested adding core-strengthening exercises and encouraged us to have him take breaks from sitting every 30–45 minutes.

We tried these strategies at home for several months. While they helped somewhat (especially when we were consistent with stretching and limiting screens), the back pain and headaches have continued to come and go. We have not yet seen a physical therapist or orthopedist specifically for this issue. No prior chiropractic care has been done; your office would be his first experience with chiropractic treatment.

We also took him to his pediatric optometrist to rule out vision-related headache causes; his prescription was updated slightly, but the headaches have still persisted intermittently even with the new glasses.
current_health_conditions.what_makes_problem_better:
Several things seem to improve Ethan’s symptoms when we are diligent about them:

1. **Stretching and Movement Breaks:** When we remind him to do gentle stretches for his hamstrings, hip flexors, and lower back (child’s pose, cat-cow, and simple toe touches), his back feels noticeably better, especially after soccer or a long car ride. Also, when he takes 5–10 minute movement breaks during homework or screen time to walk around or do a few jumping jacks, his back tightness often eases.

2. **Heat and Warm Showers:** Applying a warm compress or heating pad (on a low setting, supervised) to his mid-to-lower back for 10–15 minutes helps relax the muscles. He often says his back feels “looser” after a warm shower in the evening, especially if he was sore after practice.

3. **Light Massage:** Gently massaging the muscles along either side of his spine and at the base of his neck helps reduce the tension. He frequently asks for a short back rub before bed on days when he’s had a headache or back pain, and he usually falls asleep more easily afterward.

4. **Lying Flat on His Back:** If his back is bothering him, lying flat on a firm surface (like the carpeted floor) with his knees bent and feet flat often brings relief within 10–20 minutes. Sometimes he will place a small rolled towel under his knees, which seems comfortable for him.

5. **Limiting Screen Time and Good Posture:** On days when we closely monitor his screen time and make sure he sits upright with his feet on the floor and the screen at eye level, his headaches are less frequent or less intense. If he uses his tablet at the kitchen table instead of slouching on the couch, we notice fewer complaints.

6. **Occasional Children’s Acetaminophen:** When headaches are more bothersome (around a 5–6 out of 10), giving a single weight-appropriate dose of children’s acetaminophen usually helps within 30–45 minutes. We try to reserve this for when rest, hydration, and a snack haven’t helped enough.

Overall, consistent stretching, posture awareness, warmth, and brief rest periods seem to make the biggest difference in reducing his discomfort and preventing the headaches from escalating.
current_health_conditions.what_makes_problem_worse:
The symptoms tend to flare up under several predictable circumstances:

1. **Extended Screen Time with Poor Posture:** If Ethan spends more than an hour at a time on his Chromebook, Nintendo Switch, or watching videos on a tablet—especially when he is curled up on the couch or lying on his stomach—his neck and upper back tighten up and a headache often follows within a couple of hours. We’ve noticed that if he plays Minecraft or watches YouTube for long stretches without breaks, he is almost guaranteed to complain of a headache by evening.

2. **Long Periods of Sitting at School or in the Car:** On days with a lot of seated classroom work, testing, or long car rides (like when we drive to visit his grandparents in Colorado Springs), his mid-to-lower back tends to get stiff and sore. He often says his back feels “stuck” after a long drive, and he bends forward or twists around in his seat trying to get comfortable.

3. **Intense Soccer Practices and Tournaments:** When he has multiple games in one day or a weekend tournament, especially if he is playing goalie and diving or landing on his side and back, the following day his lower and mid-back are usually more painful. Running sprints on hard turf also seems to aggravate things, particularly if he is already tired and not focusing on form.

4. **Carrying a Heavy Backpack:** If he loads his backpack with several textbooks, his Chromebook, and his lunchbox and then walks home from the bus stop, he sometimes complains of low back discomfort by the time he gets home. We’ve noticed that he tends to wear his backpack low on his hips with only one strap tightened, which likely strains his back and shoulders.

5. **Dehydration and Skipping Snacks:** On busy days when he doesn’t drink enough water or skips his afternoon snack, his headaches are more likely and often worse. His soccer coach has mentioned that he sometimes forgets to drink from his water bottle during practice, and on those evenings he frequently reports both a headache and feeling “worn out.”

6. **Stress and Fatigue:** On weeks with more homework, tests, or when he goes to bed later than usual, his headaches seem to increase in frequency. He tends to clench his jaw and tighten his shoulders when he’s anxious, which we suspect contributes to the neck and upper back tension that triggers the headaches.

health_goals_for_your_child.top_health_goal_1: Our primary goal is to reduce or ideally eliminate Ethan’s recurring back pain and tension-type headaches so that he can comfortably participate in school, sports, and family activities without frequently needing medication or having to sit out due to discomfort. We want him to be able to play soccer, sit through class, and do his homework without constantly shifting around or complaining that his back or head hurts.
health_goals_for_your_child.top_health_goal_2: We want to improve Ethan’s posture and spinal alignment, especially given how much time he spends sitting at school and using screens. We would like guidance and a concrete plan to help him develop healthier sitting, standing, and sleeping habits now, so that he doesn’t carry poor posture—and the associated pain—into his teenage years and adulthood. Ideally, we’d like to see his shoulders more relaxed and back, his head better aligned over his spine, and less frequent slouching when he reads or uses devices.
health_goals_for_your_child.top_health_goal_3: We hope to support Ethan’s overall wellness and physical development by improving his flexibility, core strength, and body awareness. We would appreciate home exercises or stretches tailored to him that we can realistically incorporate into our daily routine. We also want education for both Ethan and us as parents about how to prevent future issues related to sports, growth spurts, and screen use, so that chiropractic care can be part of a proactive, long-term wellness plan rather than just something we turn to when he is in pain.

pregnancy__fertility_history.mother_exercise_explanation:
During Ethan’s pregnancy, I (Laura) maintained a moderate exercise routine with my OB’s approval. Before pregnancy I was a regular runner, so during the first and second trimesters I transitioned to brisk walking 4–5 times per week for about 30–40 minutes at a time. I also attended a prenatal yoga class once a week from around 16 weeks until about 34 weeks, which focused on gentle stretching, breathing, and hip-opening poses.

In the third trimester, I reduced the intensity and frequency slightly due to fatigue and some mild lower back discomfort. At that point, I typically walked 3 times per week for 20–30 minutes and did simple stretching at home on non-walking days. I avoided high-impact activities and any exercises my OB advised against. Overall, the exercise helped me manage stress, sleep better, and keep my weight gain within the recommended range.
pregnancy__fertility_history.mother_ill_explanation:
Overall, the pregnancy was healthy, but I did experience a few minor illnesses and complications:

- **First Trimester Nausea:** I had fairly typical morning sickness from about weeks 6–12, with nausea throughout the day and occasional vomiting. I was able to manage it with small, frequent meals, ginger tea, and rest. No IV fluids or hospitalizations were needed.

- **Mild Iron-Deficiency Anemia:** Around the start of the third trimester, routine bloodwork showed that my iron levels were slightly low. My OB recommended an iron supplement in addition to my prenatal vitamin and encouraged me to increase iron-rich foods (spinach, red meat, beans). Follow-up labs showed improvement, and there were no major complications.

- **Seasonal Allergies and a Mild Cold:** I had one mild upper respiratory infection around 24 weeks (runny nose, sore throat, low-grade fever for one day). I managed it with rest, fluids, and OB-approved medications (saline nasal spray, acetaminophen for the brief fever). No antibiotics were required. I also had my usual spring seasonal allergies, which were controlled with saline rinses and staying indoors on high-pollen days.

There were no issues with gestational diabetes, high blood pressure, preeclampsia, or serious infections. Fetal growth and movement were consistently normal at all prenatal visits.
pregnancy__fertility_history.any_ultrasounds_explanation:
I had three standard ultrasounds during Ethan’s pregnancy:

1. **Early Dating Ultrasound (around 8 weeks):** This was done to confirm the pregnancy, establish accurate dating, and verify that it was a single intrauterine pregnancy. Everything looked normal.

2. **Anatomy Scan (around 20 weeks):** This detailed ultrasound checked Ethan’s organs, spine, brain, heart, and overall development. The technician and the OB said everything appeared normal and on track for his gestational age. They did not identify any structural abnormalities or concerns.

3. **Third-Trimester Growth Check (around 32 weeks):** My OB ordered a follow-up ultrasound to confirm growth because my fundal height was measuring slightly ahead. The scan showed that Ethan was on the higher end of normal for weight but still within a healthy range. Amniotic fluid levels were normal, and there were no concerns about his position or the placenta.

All ultrasounds were routine, with no additional specialized imaging or high-risk pregnancy monitoring required.
pregnancy__fertility_history.pregnancy_stress_episodes:
During the first half of the pregnancy, I was working full-time as an elementary school teacher, and the combination of long days on my feet, morning sickness, and classroom demands did create periods of physical and emotional stress. I remember feeling especially overwhelmed during parent–teacher conference week when I was about 14–16 weeks along, as I was staying late at school and not sleeping as well.

Around the start of the third trimester, my father had a minor heart procedure (angioplasty). Although the doctors were reassuring and everything went smoothly, the lead-up to the procedure and a few weeks afterward were stressful emotionally. I traveled to see him once, but my OB cleared me for the short trip, and I took extra care to rest and stay hydrated.

Aside from those episodes, I would describe the pregnancy as moderately stressful in the normal sense—balancing work, finances, and preparing for our first child—but there were no severe or traumatic events such as accidents, major illnesses, or significant relationship crises. I had regular prenatal care and a supportive partner and family, which helped a great deal in managing the day-to-day stress.
pregnancy__fertility_history.other_concerns_conception_pregnancy:
Conception was fairly straightforward; we conceived Ethan after about four months of trying, with no fertility treatments needed. My cycles were regular, and my OB did not identify any underlying reproductive issues.

During pregnancy, the main additional concern I had was related to my lower back. I had occasional mild low back discomfort even before pregnancy, especially after long days of teaching, and this did flare up somewhat in the third trimester as my belly grew and my posture changed. My OB recommended a maternity support belt, continued gentle exercise, and proper body mechanics when lifting or standing for long periods. I followed those recommendations and never required physical therapy during the pregnancy, but I have always wondered if my pre-existing tendency toward low back tightness might have some connection to Ethan’s current posture and back issues.

I also gained about 32 pounds total, which was slightly above the lower end of the recommended range for my pre-pregnancy BMI but still considered acceptable by my OB. There were no restrictions placed on my activity level beyond the usual pregnancy precautions. Labor and delivery were relatively uncomplicated: spontaneous labor at 39 weeks, vaginal delivery, epidural anesthesia, and Ethan had good Apgar scores. He did not require NICU care and came home with us on the standard postpartum timeline.
